thai: ขนมปังนมอบนุ่ม

food_subgroup: Steamed or baked bread buns with milk filling or milk-enriched dough

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Thai steamed milk buns. FOODON_03315116 (bread product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Thai steamed milk buns.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Thai steamed milk buns. SNOMED CT coverage for this specific product category is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 buns weighing approximately 160 g in total (approximately 40 g per bun), as sold by the restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bread, white, commercially prepared (includes soft bread rolls)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai steamed milk buns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for white soft bread rolls (FDC ID 353479) is used as the closest defensible analogue. Values are adapted to reflect the milk-enriched, slightly sweeter dough typical of Thai milk buns. Sugar and fat values are adjusted upward slightly to account for added milk and butter content. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — White bread (soft rolls)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai steamed milk buns. GI of 71 is assigned by analogy to white soft bread rolls as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 86.4) / 100 = 61.3, rounded to 61.6 using 54.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 160 g serving = 86.4 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.guidance: Each 160 g serving of Milky Way Buns provides approximately 608 mg sodium, representing about 26% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control is advised and pairing with low-sodium accompaniments is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and high glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 71 (high) and a glycemic load of approximately 61.6 per 4-bun serving, Milky Way Buns can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, consider consuming only 1–2 buns, and pair with protein or fibre-rich foods to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 4.5 g saturated fat and 608 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency and choose lower-fat, lower-sodium bread alternatives as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milky Way Buns are soft, pillowy steamed or baked bread buns made from a milk-enriched, slightly sweetened wheat dough. Sold in portions of 4 buns per serving, they are a popular bakery-style item offered at Thai and Southeast Asian restaurants in Singapore, including at Shrimp Prawn Seafood – Boat Quay. The buns have a tender, fluffy crumb and a lightly golden or pale exterior, with a mild milky sweetness and a soft, slightly chewy texture. They may be served plain or with a light glaze. The dough is typically made from wheat flour, fresh milk or condensed milk, sugar, butter or margarine, eggs, and yeast or baking powder. Nutritionally, each 160 g serving (4 buns) provides approximately 477 kcal, 12.8 g protein, 8.8 g fat, and 86.4 g carbohydrate. The high carbohydrate content and high glycemic index (GI ~71) make these buns a significant source of rapidly available glucose, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 608 mg per serving, which is relevant for hypertension management. The buns contain gluten, milk, and eggs and are not suitable for individuals with these allergens. They are vegetarian and generally considered halal. In the context of Thai cuisine in Singapore, milk buns represent a fusion of Western bakery technique with Southeast Asian flavour preferences, often served as a side dish or snack alongside seafood meals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Milky Way Buns?
What is the GI of milk buns?
Are milk buns suitable for diabetics?
What are Thai milk buns made of?
How much sodium in steamed milk buns?
What allergens are in milk buns?
How many carbs in 4 milk buns?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Thai steamed milk buns exists in any primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy white soft bread rolls, adjusted for the milk-enriched, sweeter dough profile. GI is estimated by analogy to white soft bread rolls from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 353479, white soft bread rolls) with upward adjustment to sugar_g and fat_g to reflect the milk-enriched dough typical of Thai milk buns. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors (4 kcal/g protein, 9 kcal/g fat, 4 kcal/g carbohydrate). 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero — white bread products contain negligible vitamin C, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source for this product category; value is absent from the source database entry. 4. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source for this product category; value is absent from the source database entry. 5. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source for this product category; GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white soft bread rolls from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Thai steamed milk buns was found in approved sources. 15. Serving size of 160 g (4 × 40 g buns) is estimated based on typical bakery bun weights; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 16. The food is listed under Thai cuisine as tagged in the field survey source record, though milk buns of this style are common across Southeast Asian bakery traditions. 17. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches only; no exact ontology code exists for this specific product.

Rewrote all -1.0 sentinel value notes to explicitly state 'not reported in the adapted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source for this product category; value is absent from the source database entry' for each of: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g. Each note now names the source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) and states the reason (not reported / absent from source). This satisfies the g3 sentinel values documentation rule requiring explicit reason and source for every -1 value.
